item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
creatures straight from your worst nightmares for no less than ten millennia!

The history of mankind has been going on for around 12000 years. There's a possibility for these creatures to be humans, right?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: No matter what it implies, the twist ending gave me a good chuckle, definitely +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It's also possible the Probe means something else other than the common meaning of 365ish rotations of the Earth. But this is not a mistake they would have made if they could speak with fluency all the major languages of man and keep abreast of linguistic evolution, nor would they have a reason to be deliberately ambiguous about the time unit being used.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm just going to say it now, it was either the Daevites or the Sasquatch who got the answer wrong last time, with different implications for each possibility.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Dang that's a nice analysis. Maybe if the object… used solar power to constantly, forcefully keep it synced to earth's orbit despite its mass difference?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Oh shit, what if. It's not SCPs plaguing humanity for answering wrong. It's humanity taking over the yetis cuz they got it wrong. And by replacing them like this, the 2nd attempt to answer the question will be different.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Noticed a slight grammatical error in:

The image features six objects. It is hypothesized that image is schematic model of the Solar Sytem

Fixed the Sytem to System.

All in all a very good SCP. Although the punchline was kinda obvious from the start it does make one wonder what manner of civilization there was so far back to manage to attempt an answer, and what happened to them. It's a + from me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: That was a pretty great plot twist. This is way too underwhelming and silly for me to believe it's really the source of any/all anomalies though, so my headcanon is that there never actually was a question and this thing hasn't done anything to the Earth. We're just being trolled by some jackass aliens.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm another reader who thinks this best describes the curse of humans upon the Earth (retroactively applied, of course).
Also, one fun note: to be opposite Earth's orbit and mirroring it, the SCP would need to match Earth's Mass! With the given dimensions, if 2092 were made of solid neutronium it would have 2.24x1020 kg, but the Earth's mass is 5.972×1024 kg! This thing is (somehow) denser than neutronium, and has resisted collapsing into a sphere. Just another subtle anomalous trait.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
considering that the 'cooldown' for the riddle is stated to be no less than 10000 years, that means this would have been received in 6000 BCE.

Yes. That would be the implication.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I think you should increase what the timer at the bottom says. It currently says 2000 years, and considering that the 'cooldown' for the riddle is stated to be no less than 10000 years, that means this would have been received in 6000 BCE. I would say that we should change it to around 9985 years.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Heh. This could be an 001 proposal, I guess. Although there's plenty of things that it wouldn't explain, like the teleforce. I like it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Terrific. Four thumbs up.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I didn't like it that much, mainly because the Ambassador's message seemed very over-the-top hammy, and the end twist could be foreseen a mile away.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: ohhhhh shit! +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Thanks for the suggestion, I'm rewriting that sentence.
Regarding your remark about the start corner, one can notice that we would get a rotated or reflected version of the original image by starting at another corner. This picture would convey the same meaning. (I used a method of transmission and a type of image that works regardless of such isometries.)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: i have a feeling i missed something big after reading the comments, but i liked it, so it gets +1 from me

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I feel this would cross over beyond my threshold for "safe scientific corrections", but

The SCP-2092-1 message is a binary code of length 5329

is some really awkward phrasing. Firstly, length, even in binary, needs units. In this case, it'd be bits. Secondly, the word code denotes a representation, not a string of bits.I'd suggest:

SCP-2092-1 appears to be a messaged encoded in binary with 5329 bits of length

There's also a few extra details about how data is encoded for correct, meaningful transmission, but I'm guessing that can handwaved in "they fire this signal in periodic bursts separated by silence", whether explicitly or implicitly. There's also the minor detail that arrangement into square rows with the origin on whichever corner it starts is somewhat arbitrary, but that's perhaps nitpicking too much.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The headcanon is the reader's individual interpretation of the SCP universe. The articles in the site offer a possibility to the audience to speculate about lots of things, so everyone can develop an own headcanon. (For example you can believe that this SCP is responsible for all/some/none of the anomalies, and even consider that the human race is the punishment itself as Dax_symbiont pointed out.)

Headcannons (with double n), are a different thing.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I apologise for my ignorance but what is a headcannon? Does that just mean it doesnt quite fit with what we believe of the world? I look up the definition but I find it easier asking people than Google :P

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Fascinating, I have a weakness for advanced alien ambassadors waiting for us to join them. I liked the map, but I think it needs a little fleshing out in how they discovered the information resolved into an image. For example, a couple of tests where they try to decode it into words, then sounds, then images maybe.

I really, really, really, really like this. I'm not sure if I should vote because I am extremely biased in this field XD Personally I love it <3

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Well you can start by assuming that the Objects is located quite Close to the third Earth/sun Lagrange point. The pictur indicates that, and on that position it would be in an (unstable) balance between gravitation and zentrifugal force.

then you can narrow the Possition down to quite a small region of space in wich you start triangulating.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
You got the year wrong. The message does not mean that someone messed up the answer 2352 years ago. Since the wait between two answers is 10000 years, it means that they answered 10000-2352=7645 years ago, in 5645 BC. (The message was recorded in 2000.)

I think that allowing the reader to speculate about who answered back then and what happened after that is part of the fun here, and adding a date corresponding to a real event would deny this.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Felt like a simplistic 001 which leaves a bit of a bad aftertaste, although I noticed that it just said nightmarish creatures and not all anomalous phenomena. But I loved the whiplash from "cliche alien object" to "….shit"

EDIT: Nothing much happened in http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/352_BC (October 13, according to Wolfram Alpha) … maybe adjust the date a bit to coincide with some historical reference?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Who says it was a civ? Could've just been some jackass(es) with a magic rocket, lord knows we have enough random ancient spacefaring groups to handle that.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This was an OK scip until I read this. Now, upvoted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This basically boils down to "aliens did it" and "mysterious ancient civilization dun goofed" and neither concept does anything for me. I'm especially annoyed at the "mysterious ancient civ" bit, since there would be traces of them in the historical/archaeological record. For fuck's sake, we know things about human development hundreds of thousands of years ago; there would be something left of a civ advanced enough to develop space flight less than 10kya ago.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Headcanon: This isn't aliens inviting us to the galactic community; it's aliens trolling us.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I interpreted the horrors as people, not SCPs. No human was capable of reaching that location, as far as we know, at that time. Thus, no record remains of whoever did. It might of been SCP-1000 or similar.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I feel passages in the description like

The fact that SCP-2092-1 was never received by any device in the proximity of Earth seems to support this hypothesis, since the Sun would block all electromagnetic signals from such a source.

need some ironing out (it's very "Hey, this is how the skip works, in case you didn't know") and I ultimately downvoted for the addenda. I kind of feel like we've done this before either way.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Huh. For whatever reasons, I didn't react as the commenters above did; it pretty much exactly fit my expectations.

I'm waffling on my vote; this article is exceptionally well-written (not just well-written enough to reach the rather low bar required to succeed on this site), and there's something to be said for a punchy, succinctly written SCP with good use of imagery. And the concept is not bad. And I did chuckle.

But there just isn't all that much here and I've seen it before.

No vote for now; maybe I'll come back and downvote or upvote. Either way, not bad.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I have to nth the "possible origin of other SCPs" idea as not being for me, but the expectations you set up and then execute in a really clever way help sell this for me. That note at the end threw me for a loop. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Would they actually be able to triangulate the location of something only a couple meters across in the vastness of space? I genuinely don't know; space tech isn't my thing.

That aside, while I don't know that I would headcanon this as the source of anomalies, and I don't quite follow the logic of this ambassador, that twist there — the brutal subversion of the audience's expectations, followed by a bit of a sinking feeling as the implications set in — was wonderfully executed, and that makes the whole thing work for me. +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The "possible origin of other SCPs" gimmick I didn't really like, but as I said in the forums I enjoyed the twist in the ending. +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It's like it's trying to be an 001 proposal, but it lacks any of the emotional punch/complex story/ plot twist/complete background necessary to be a true 001, hence its relegation to mainlist status.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: You should not worry about colliding with other headcanons, really. They are called headcanons for a reason. You perceive the Foundation's origin and purpose in one way, but that shouldn't be a reason for someone to dislike your work just because they have a different way of seeing an outcome or a beginning for the Foundationverse. In addition, headcanons are hardly ever the same.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: You did not comment on the Drafts thread, but thank you for doing it now. I fixed the GHz issue and removed the word 'round'. You got the implication right, I wanted to imply that without stating it directly (since it would collide with lots of headcanons).

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I remember reading this once, and i think i dished out feedback on it, but i'm going to do it again.

When talking about frequency, as soon as you reach the thousand threshold on the megahertz unit, you switch it to gigahertz.

As it is, it does nothing for me. It's a combination of the Hateful Star and SCP-2716 and i do think that they pulled this off in a better way than this. I do like the implication that someone got the question wrong and that's the reason we have a lot of egregious things in the Foundationverse, but that's about it. There are some flaws in the description - you end up leaving some things to the imagination of the reader and also explaining a lot of things that you could have implied instead.

It's not bad, but i really don't feel it because the similarities with the two articles i mentioned above are really strong. The only thing i liked is the implication of the origin of the anomalies the Foundation currently contains, if i got that right. So, no vote.

Lastly, there are five round objects in the image, not six. The far left thing is a square, at least for me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
New SCP. Thanks to everyone who helped with the draft.

Image source
